title: IME composition state read before compositionend fires
description: Input/change handlers act on text or fire side effects while an IME composition (kana-to-kanji conversion) is still in progress, instead of waiting for compositionend. Pressing Enter to confirm a conversion then also triggers whatever handler is bound to Enter or change, such as a form submit.
minimal_repro: input.addEventListener('keydown', e => { if (e.key === 'Enter') submit(); }); // fires mid-conversion, no isComposing guard
detection: Track compositionstart/compositionend explicitly, or read event.isComposing (native DOM) / event.nativeEvent.isComposing (React synthetic events) / keyCode 229 as a legacy fallback. Test with a real CJK IME enabled, not by pasting already-converted text.
fix: Guard the handler: if (e.isComposing || e.keyCode === 229) return; and only act on the input/keydown that fires after compositionend.
source_quality: primary - real, open GitHub issue against React

title: Zenkaku/hankaku (full-width/half-width) mismatch breaks equality and search
description: Japanese input mixes full-width forms (１２３, ＡＢＣ) and half-width forms (123, ABC). Code that compares or indexes user text without normalizing width first treats visually similar strings as different.
minimal_repro: '１' === '1' // false - a search index keyed on half-width digits misses full-width input
detection: Audit every place user-entered text is compared or indexed without a normalization step first. The library's own issue tracker shows how easy it is to mix up which flag (ascii / digit / kana) normalizes which character class.
fix: Normalize with String.prototype.normalize('NFKC') before comparison or indexing, or use a purpose-built converter (jaconv, utsuho) with flags matched to the character classes actually present.
source_quality: primary - real closed issue on jaconv describing ascii/digit/kana flag confusion in the library's own README example

title: CJK line breaks land on forbidden characters (kinsoku shori violations)
description: CJK text has no spaces to hint word breaks, so default browser line-breaking can wrap anywhere - including before closing punctuation such as 。、」）, which Japanese kinsoku shori rules forbid from starting a line.
minimal_repro:
これはカーニングのルールを無視する例。

with no line-break/word-break CSS set - punctuation can orphan onto the next line's start.
detection: Visual regression test at multiple viewport widths with real Japanese paragraphs, not lorem ipsum. Flag any line that starts with 。、」） or a closing bracket without its opening pair on the same line.
fix: Apply CSS line-break: strict and word-break: keep-all where supported, or use a model-based segmenter (BudouX) to insert <wbr> at linguistically correct break points, matching the boundary classes defined by UAX #14.
source_quality: primary - UAX #14 is the normative Unicode spec; BudouX is Google's shipped OSS implementation for CJK

title: Hardcoded English strings leak into a localized UI
description: A component ships literal text ("Submit", "Loading...") instead of a translation key, so it renders in English even when the rest of the UI is in Japanese.
detection: Static lint rule that flags JSX/template text nodes not wrapped in a translation call.
fix: Route every user-facing string through the i18n library's translate function or component, and fail CI when a new raw text node is introduced.
frameworks: React/i18next (analogous plugins exist for Vue, Angular)
source_quality: primary - real, maintained ESLint plugin whose stated purpose is exactly this check

title: Locale JSON files fall out of sync (missing or orphaned keys)
description: A pull request adds a new key to en.json for a new feature but never updates ja.json, so the Japanese build renders an empty string, the raw key, or a fallback in the wrong language.
minimal_repro: en.json has "confirm_delete"; ja.json does not - t('confirm_delete') returns undefined or the literal key in the ja build.
detection: CI step that diffs the key sets of every locale file against the base locale and fails on any mismatch, missing or orphaned.
fix: Generate a TypeScript union type from the base locale's keys so t() calls are type-checked, and add a CI diff gate before merge.
source_quality: primary - official library documentation for the type-generation approach used to prevent this drift

title: Missing lang attribute or CJK font stack renders tofu boxes
description: Without a correct lang attribute or a CJK-aware font-family fallback, browsers can render Japanese text as empty boxes (tofu) or pick a Chinese glyph variant for a Han-unified character, changing its shape.
minimal_repro:
今日は良い天気です。

with no and a font stack that ends before any CJK font, on an OS without the matching system font installed.
detection: Render the page in a clean OS/browser profile with no CJK fonts pre-installed and screenshot-diff against a reference; check lang attributes with an HTML validator.
fix: Set <html lang="ja"> (or a scoped lang on the CJK subtree) and include an explicit CJK web font, e.g. Noto Sans JP, in the font stack rather than relying on system fallback.
source_quality: primary - W3C Internationalization Activity's own guidance page on why lang matters, including font/glyph selection

title: RTL text breaks layout when the app only styles for LTR
description: Arabic and Hebrew content mirror reading direction, but a codebase using physical CSS properties (margin-left, text-align: left) instead of logical ones renders punctuation and alignment on the wrong side once dir="rtl" is set.
minimal_repro: .card { margin-left: 16px; } next to RTL-language content - the margin lands on the visual right instead of the layout start.
detection: RTL visual regression test suite, or a stylelint rule banning physical properties (left/right) in favor of logical ones (inline-start/inline-end).
fix: Replace physical CSS properties with logical properties, or transform an existing LTR stylesheet with a tool such as RTLCSS.
source_quality: primary - real, actively maintained tool (1.7k+ stars) built specifically to solve this class of bug

title: Ternary pluralization breaks for languages with more than two plural forms
description: count === 1 ? 'file' : 'files' only encodes English's two plural categories. Languages such as Arabic have up to six plural categories (zero/one/two/few/many/other); a naive ternary mis-grammars them or silently falls back to English-shaped logic.
minimal_repro: `${count} file${count === 1 ? '' : 's'}` applied to a string that gets translated into a language whose plural grammar does not match English's binary split.
detection: Lint or review rule flagging manual plural ternaries around translated strings; snapshot-test rendered plural strings for every configured locale.
fix: Use ICU MessageFormat plural blocks (react-intl/FormatJS or i18next) driven by CLDR plural rules for each locale, instead of hand-written conditionals.
source_quality: primary - FormatJS docs are the library's own reference; CLDR is the canonical plural-rule data source it consumes

title: Manual date/number formatting ignores locale, including Japanese era dates
description: Concatenating date or number strings by hand, or calling toLocaleDateString() without a locale, produces output in the wrong format (currency symbol, digit grouping, calendar) for the user's locale - including 和暦 (Japanese era, e.g. 令和) dates that a plain Gregorian formatter cannot produce.
minimal_repro: new Date().toLocaleDateString() with no arguments renders using the runtime's default locale, not the user's; a hand-rolled `${y}年${m}月${d}日` string never accounts for era names.
detection: Snapshot-test formatted output for every supported locale, including at least one non-Gregorian calendar case (ja-JP-u-ca-japanese).
fix: Use Intl.DateTimeFormat / Intl.NumberFormat with an explicit locale argument, passing calendar: 'japanese' where era-based dates are required, instead of building strings manually.
frameworks: JavaScript Intl (equivalents: Python babel, Java time + Locale)
source_quality: primary - MDN's own Intl reference documents the locale-aware formatters and calendar option that fix this class of bug

title: Mismatched charset declaration or serving produces mojibake
description: A server serves Shift-JIS or EUC-JP bytes while the client assumes UTF-8, or vice versa, or an HTML document is missing <meta charset>, so Japanese text renders as garbled characters (mojibake) instead of failing loudly.
minimal_repro: An HTML response with no charset in the Content-Type header and no <meta charset="utf-8"> tag, serving bytes encoded as Shift-JIS, opened in a browser that defaults to UTF-8.
detection: CI check that every HTML response or file explicitly declares UTF-8 (HTTP header and/or meta tag), and that source files are saved as UTF-8, not a legacy Japanese encoding.
fix: Standardize all I/O, database columns, and HTML documents on UTF-8, and declare it explicitly at every layer (HTTP header, meta tag, DB connection charset) instead of relying on defaults.
source_quality: primary - W3C Internationalization Activity's own guidance on encoding declarations

title: A stray byte-order-mark corrupts the first field of parsed text
description: Some tools, notably Excel-exported CSV, prepend a UTF-8 byte-order-mark (EF BB BF) to text files. Code that reads the file without stripping it ends up with the BOM glued to the first field name or first character, so the first row or column silently fails to match anything.
minimal_repro: A CSV file starting with \ufeffname,age - a naive header === 'name' check fails only for the first column.
detection: Byte-level check for EF BB BF at file start in the parsing/import pipeline's test fixtures; test with a file actually exported from Excel or Windows Notepad, not a hand-written UTF-8 file.
fix: Strip a leading BOM before parsing (text.replace(/^\ufeff/, '')), or use a CSV/text parser that already strips it, applied consistently everywhere the file is read.
source_quality: primary - Unicode Consortium's own FAQ on BOM behavior and pitfalls

title: Code that walks text by UTF-16 code unit splits multi-code-point graphemes
description: Cursor movement, truncation, or backspace logic implemented with code-unit or code-point indexing instead of grapheme clusters can split a surrogate pair, separate a combining mark from its base character, or break an Indic conjunct cluster that should behave as one visual unit.
minimal_repro: Deleting one visual character from a string containing a combining mark or conjunct cluster removes only part of the cluster, leaving a broken glyph behind.
detection: Test cursor/backspace/truncate operations against Intl.Segmenter-derived grapheme counts as the oracle, using real multi-code-point test strings, not ASCII fixtures.
fix: Use a grapheme-cluster-aware segmenter (Intl.Segmenter with granularity: 'grapheme', or an equivalent library) for any operation that counts, truncates, or moves a cursor through user-facing text, instead of iterating code units or code points directly.
frameworks: any text editor / input handling (example: Slate.js)
source_quality: primary - UAX #29 is the normative spec; the Slate PR is a real, currently open (unmerged) fix against a widely used rich-text editor

title: Space-based word or reading-time counters treat a CJK paragraph as one word
description: Japanese, Chinese, and Thai text has no spaces between words, so a word counter or "N min read" estimator that splits on whitespace counts an entire CJK paragraph as a single word, producing absurd reading-time estimates.
minimal_repro: "これはテストの文章です。".split(' ').length returns 1, regardless of how many words the sentence actually contains.
detection: Compare word/reading-time output for a CJK sample against a segmenter-based count; a ratio near "paragraph length in characters divided by 1" indicates the whitespace-split bug is present.
fix: Use a locale-aware segmenter (Intl.Segmenter with granularity: 'word', or a dedicated tokenizer such as BudouX/MeCab/Kuromoji for Japanese) instead of splitting on whitespace, and derive reading-time estimates from character count for CJK locales rather than word count.
source_quality: primary - UAX #29 word-boundary rules and the MDN Segmenter API that implements them

title: Missing 特定商取引法 (Tokushoho) notation on a Japanese e-commerce checkout
description: Japan's Act on Specified Commercial Transactions requires online sellers to disclose specific business information (operator name, address, phone, return policy, price/fees) before a Japanese consumer completes a purchase. A checkout built to satisfy only a US-style Terms of Service page omits this disclosure entirely.
minimal_repro: A checkout flow with "Terms of Service" and "Privacy Policy" footer links but no page carrying the disclosure items required for 通信販売 (mail-order/online sales) under the Act.
detection: Manual or automated presence check for a page containing the required disclosure fields (事業者名, 所在地, 電話番号, 返品特約, 販売価格 等) linked from the Japanese checkout flow.
fix: Add a dedicated 特定商取引法に基づく表記 page listing the required fields and link it from checkout/footer for any store selling to Japanese consumers.
source_quality: primary - official Japanese government law text (e-Gov law database); this entry replaces the source corpus's original unverified flag with a primary citation

title: Default array sort produces wrong order for Japanese/CJK text
description: Array.prototype.sort() without a comparator sorts strings by UTF-16 code unit, not by linguistically correct collation order. Japanese kana, kanji readings, and mixed-script lists end up in an order that does not match how Japanese users expect an alphabetized (50音順) list to read.
minimal_repro: ['りんご','あんず','いちご'].sort() returns code-point order, which breaks as soon as the list mixes kanji, katakana, or Latin entries that code-point order does not group the way native readers expect.
detection: Compare default .sort() output against Intl.Collator output for a mixed-script test list; any difference indicates the bug is present.
fix: Sort with new Intl.Collator(locale).compare (array.sort(collator.compare)) instead of the default comparator, using the correct locale for the content being sorted.
frameworks: JavaScript Intl.Collator (equivalents: ICU collation in Java/Python/Go)
source_quality: primary - MDN's own reference for the API that fixes this
